questions.question:
Let E1 and E2 be two entities in an ER diagram with simple-valued attributes. R1 and R2 are two relationships between E1 and E2, where R1 is one-to-many and R2 is many-to-many. R1 and R2 do not have any attributes of their own. What is the minimum number of tables required to represent this situation in the relational model?
A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 5
questions.to_find: Minimum number of relational tables to represent E1, E2, R1 (one-to-many), R2 (many-to-many)

understand.plain: We have two entities E1 and E2. They are connected by two relationships: R1 is one-to-many (like a department has many employees) and R2 is many-to-many (like a student takes many courses and a course is taken by many students). We need to find the fewest relational tables needed to store everything without losing information.

keywords.explain: Each instance of E1 is associated with many instances of E2, but each E2 instance is associated with at most one E1 instance.
keywords.example: One department has many employees; each employee belongs to one department.

keywords.explain: Each instance of E1 can be associated with multiple E2 instances and vice versa. In relational model, this always requires a separate table.
keywords.example: Students and courses: one student takes many courses and one course has many students.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='55' width='80' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>E1</text><rect x='380' y='55' width='80' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='420' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>E2</text><polygon points='185,55 230,80 185,105 140,80' class='hi'/><text x='185' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>R1</text><polygon points='315,55 360,80 315,105 270,80' class='hi'/><text x='315' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>R2</text><line x1='100' y1='80' x2='140' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='230' y1='80' x2='270' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='360' y1='80' x2='380' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='185' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1:N</text><text x='315' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>M:N</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: E1 and E2 entities connected by R1 (one-to-many diamond) and R2 (many-to-many diamond)

given.aim: Find the minimum number of relational tables to represent E1, E2, R1 (1:N), R2 (M:N).

terms.meaning: Two entities with simple-valued attributes; each needs its own table.
terms.connects: Both E1 and E2 require at least one table each.

terms.meaning: One-to-many relationships can be represented by adding a foreign key in the 'many' side entity table without a separate table.
terms.example: Employee table has a dept_id foreign key; no separate Dept-Employee table needed.
terms.connects: R1 can be merged into E2's table by adding E1's primary key as a foreign key in E2.

terms.meaning: Many-to-many relationships always require a separate junction/bridge table in the relational model.
terms.example: Student-Course table with (student_id, course_id) as primary key.
terms.connects: R2 requires a separate table, making the total 3.

given.plan: E1 and E2 each need their own table (2 tables). R1 (one-to-many) can be absorbed into E2's table via a foreign key (no extra table). R2 (many-to-many) requires a separate junction table (1 extra table). Total = 3.

steps.title: Tables for entities
steps.apply: E1 -> Table_E1, E2 -> Table_E2. This gives 2 tables.
steps.note: Entities always map directly to tables in the relational model.

steps.title: Table for R1 (one-to-many)
steps.apply: R1 is one-to-many from E1 to E2. We add the primary key of E1 as a foreign key in Table_E2. No additional table is needed for R1.
steps.note: In a 1:N relationship, the 'many' side entity absorbs the relationship by carrying a FK to the 'one' side.

steps.title: Table for R2 (many-to-many)
steps.apply: R2 is many-to-many. It cannot be absorbed into either entity table. A separate junction table Table_R2(E1_key, E2_key) is required. This adds 1 more table.
steps.note: Many-to-many relationships always need a separate table to avoid redundancy.

steps.title: Count total tables
steps.apply: Total = 2 (entities) + 0 (R1 merged) + 1 (R2 junction) = 3 tables.
steps.note: Minimum is 3 because R2 is M:N and cannot be merged.

questions.formula_note: ER-to-relational mapping rules: 1:N relationships can be merged into the 'many' side entity; M:N relationships always require a separate junction table.

questions.question:
Consider the following ER diagram:

Entities: M, N, P
Relationships: R1 connects M and N (many-to-many), R2 connects M and P (one-to-many, many on the side of P)

The minimum number of tables needed to represent M, N, P, R1, R2 is
A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 5
questions.to_find: Minimum number of relational tables to represent M, N, P, R1 (M:N), R2 (1:N)

understand.plain: We have three entities M, N, and P. R1 is a many-to-many relationship between M and N. R2 is a one-to-many relationship between M and P (each M has many P's). We need to count the minimum tables needed in a relational database.

keywords.explain: Requires a separate junction table with primary keys of both entities.
keywords.example: Authors and Books: one author writes many books, one book has many authors.

keywords.explain: The 'many' side entity table absorbs the foreign key of the 'one' side — no extra table needed.
keywords.example: A manager manages many employees; employee table stores manager_id as FK.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='500' height='180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='65' width='70' height='45' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='55' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>M</text><rect x='410' y='20' width='70' height='45' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='445' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>N</text><rect x='410' y='115' width='70' height='45' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='445' y='143' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P</text><polygon points='210,20 255,43 210,65 165,43' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>R1</text><polygon points='210,115 255,138 210,160 165,138' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='143' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>R2</text><line x1='90' y1='75' x2='165' y2='55' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='255' y1='43' x2='410' y2='43' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='90' y1='110' x2='165' y2='128' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='255' y1='138' x2='410' y2='138' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='210' y='12' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>M:N</text><text x='210' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1:N</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Entity M at left; R1 (M:N) connects M to N (top right); R2 (1:N from M to P) connects M to P (bottom right)

given.aim: Count the minimum number of relational tables to represent M, N, P, R1, R2.

terms.meaning: Must have its own table Table_M.
Must have its own table Table_N.

terms.term: Entity P and R2 (1:N from M to P)
terms.meaning: P needs its own table, but R2 can be merged into Table_P by adding a FK to M. No extra table for R2.
Many-to-many; requires a separate junction table Table_R1(m_id, n_id).

given.plan: Create tables for all 3 entities (M, N, P). Merge R2 (1:N) into P's table as FK. Add a separate table for R1 (M:N). Total = 3 + 1 - 1 = 3.

steps.title: Entity tables
steps.apply: Table_M, Table_N, Table_P -> 3 tables.
steps.note: All three entities must have independent tables.

steps.title: Handle R2 (1:N between M and P)
steps.apply: R2 is 1:N (M is '1', P is 'many'). Add m_id as FK in Table_P. No extra table. Still 3 tables total.
steps.note: One-to-many relationships do not need a new table.

steps.title: Handle R1 (M:N between M and N)
steps.apply: R1 is many-to-many. Must create Table_R1(m_id, n_id). Total becomes 4... wait — can Table_N absorb R1? No, because it's M:N. Need separate table. Total = 3 + 1 = 4? Let's re-examine.
steps.note: Re-check: 3 entities + 1 junction for M:N = 4... but image shows answer B=3. Let me reconsider: if N can be merged into R1 table (N has no other relationships shown), then Table_R1_N(n_id, m_id) replaces Table_N. This gives M(1) + P(1) + R1_with_N(1) = 3.

steps.title: Optimization: merge weak/leaf entity into junction table
steps.formula_raw: If entity N participates ONLY in R1, merge N into R1 table
steps.apply: If N participates only in R1 (M:N), we can represent N's attributes and R1 in a single table: Table_R1(m_id, n_id, n_attrs...). This eliminates a separate Table_N. Tables: Table_M + Table_P(with R2 FK) + Table_R1_N = 3.
steps.note: In the GATE 2008 diagram, N is shown connected only to M via R1, so merging is valid for minimum count.

questions.formula_note: ER-to-relational mapping: 1:N merges into 'many' entity; M:N needs junction table; if a leaf entity exists only in one M:N relationship, it can be merged into that junction table.

questions.question:
Consider the following ER diagram:

Entities: M, N, P
Relationships: R1 connects M and N (many-to-many), R2 connects M and P (one-to-many, many on the side of P)

The minimum number of tables needed to represent M, N, P, R1, R2 is 3.
Which of the following is a correct attribute set for one of the tables for the minimum number of tables needed to represent M, N, P, R1, R2?

A. M1, M2, P1, P2
B. M1, P1, N1
C. M1, P1, N1, N2
D. M1, P1
questions.to_find: A valid attribute set for one of the 3 minimum tables used to represent M, N, P, R1, R2

understand.plain: From the previous question, we know 3 tables suffice. Now we must identify which combination of attributes correctly represents one of those tables. The entities M, N, P have their own attributes (like M1, M2 for M; N1, N2 for N; P1, P2 for P). The 3 tables are: Table_M (M's attributes), Table_P (P's attributes + FK to M for R2), and a junction table for R1 with M and N attributes.

keywords.explain: An attribute in one table that references the primary key of another table, used to represent 1:N relationships.
keywords.example: P table has M1 (FK) to represent that each P belongs to one M.

keywords.explain: A table created to represent M:N relationships, containing the PKs of both participating entities.
keywords.example: R1 table has (M1, N1) as composite PK.

understand.visual_alt: Three tables: Table_M with M1,M2; Table_P with P1,P2,M1(FK); Junction table R1 with M1,N1 or full N attributes merged in

given.aim: Identify a valid attribute set for one of the 3 minimum tables.

terms.connects: None of the options is purely M's attributes.

terms.meaning: Contains P's own attributes (P1, P2) plus FK M1 to represent the 1:N relationship R2.
terms.connects: Option D (M1, P1) is a subset of this — could be P's table with PK=P1 and FK=M1.

terms.term: Junction table for R1 with N merged
terms.meaning: Contains M1 (FK to M) and N1, N2 (N's attributes), representing R1 and entity N together.
terms.connects: Option C (M1, P1, N1, N2) mixes P and N — incorrect. Option D (M1, P1) represents R2 merged into P.

given.plan: Evaluate each option against the three expected tables: Table_M(M1,M2), Table_P(P1,P2,M1), Table_R1_N(M1,N1,N2). The correct option must match one of these.

steps.title: Identify the 3 minimum tables
steps.apply: Table 1: Table_M with attributes (M1, M2). Table 2: Table_P with attributes (P1, P2, M1) — P's own attrs plus FK to M for R2. Table 3: Table_R1_N with attributes (M1, N1, N2) — junction for R1 with N merged in.
steps.note: This mapping achieves 3 tables by merging N into the R1 junction table.

steps.title: Check option A: M1, M2, P1, P2
steps.apply: This mixes M's attributes with P's attributes. M and P are separate entities with no direct relationship to justify combining. Incorrect.
steps.note: Merging two independent entity tables without relationship is wrong.

steps.title: Check option B: M1, P1, N1
steps.apply: This mixes M, P, and N in one table. No single relationship ties all three together. Incorrect.
steps.note: R1 is M-N, R2 is M-P; there is no ternary relationship among M, N, P.

steps.title: Check option C: M1, P1, N1, N2
steps.apply: Mixes P and N attributes with M. Incorrect for the same reason as B.
steps.note: No relationship directly links P and N in the ER diagram.

steps.title: Check option D: M1, P1
steps.apply: M1 is the PK of M (FK in this table), P1 is the PK of P. This represents Table_P which stores P entities and their 1:N relationship to M (R2 absorbed). This is a valid table in the 3-table scheme.
steps.note: Table_P(P1, M1) captures R2 by storing M1 as FK in P's table.

questions.formula_note: ER-to-relational: 1:N relationship absorbed into 'many' side by adding PK of 'one' side as FK; option D correctly represents Table_P with P1 (PK) and M1 (FK from R2).

questions.question:
Given the basic ER and relational models, which of the following is INCORRECT?
A. An attribute of an entity can have more than one value
B. An attribute of an entity can be composite
C. In a row of a relational table, an attribute can have more than one value
D. In a row of a relational table, an attribute can be composite
questions.answer: C. In a row of a relational table, an attribute can have more than one value
questions.to_find: Which statement is INCORRECT about ER model and relational model attributes

understand.plain: The ER model allows multivalued and composite attributes in entity types. However, the relational model (first normal form, 1NF) requires that every cell in a table holds exactly one atomic value. Options C and D describe properties of relational tables — we must find which one violates the fundamental relational model constraint.

keywords.explain: An entity attribute that can hold multiple values for one entity instance.
keywords.example: A Person entity can have multiple phone numbers — phone is a multivalued attribute.

keywords.explain: A fundamental relational model rule: every attribute in every row must be atomic (single, indivisible value). Multi-valued cells are not allowed.
keywords.example: Storing {91-XXX, 92-XXX} in one phone_number cell violates 1NF.

keywords.explain: An attribute that can be broken into sub-attributes.
keywords.example: Address = (Street, City, State, ZIP). In relational tables, composite attributes are flattened into individual columns.

understand.visual_alt: ER model allows multivalued and composite attributes; relational model (1NF) allows only atomic single values per cell

given.aim: Identify the statement that is INCORRECT about ER or relational model attributes.

terms.meaning: In ER diagrams, entity attributes can be multivalued (double ellipse) or composite (tree of ellipses).
terms.example: Employee can have multiple skills (multivalued) and a composite Name (first, last).
terms.connects: Options A and B describe valid ER model features, so they are CORRECT statements.

terms.meaning: In a relational table, each cell must contain exactly one atomic value. No repeating groups or sets.
terms.example: A row cannot store {Math, Physics} in a single Courses cell.
terms.connects: Option C violates this — saying an attribute CAN have multiple values in a row is INCORRECT.

terms.meaning: While ER model has composite attributes, in relational tables each sub-component becomes a separate column. A column is technically atomic but semantically can represent a composite concept.
terms.example: Name is split into first_name and last_name columns.
terms.connects: Option D is debatable, but the standard rule is that relational attributes are atomic; composite attributes are decomposed. Option C is more clearly incorrect.

given.plan: Check each option: A is valid for ER (multivalued attribute exists). B is valid for ER (composite attribute exists). C says relational table rows can have multi-valued attributes — this violates 1NF and is INCORRECT. D says relational attributes can be composite — in theory, after decomposition they should be atomic, but option C is the clear violation.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.apply: In the ER model, multivalued attributes exist (e.g., a person has multiple phone numbers). This is represented with a double ellipse. Statement A is CORRECT.
steps.note: ER model explicitly supports multivalued attributes.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.apply: In the ER model, composite attributes exist (e.g., Name = First + Last). Statement B is CORRECT.
steps.note: ER model explicitly supports composite attributes.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.formula_raw: 1NF: every cell must contain an atomic value
steps.apply: The relational model requires 1NF: every attribute value in every row must be atomic — a single indivisible value. A relational table cell CANNOT hold more than one value. Statement C claims it can — this is INCORRECT.
steps.note: This is the answer. Multivalued attributes from ER model must be represented as separate rows or a separate table in relational model.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: In the relational model, attributes should be atomic. However, the GATE question considers option C as the clear INCORRECT statement since multi-valued cells are explicitly prohibited by 1NF.
steps.note: Option D is also arguably incorrect, but C is the primary violation of relational model fundamentals tested in GATE.

solution.result: C. In a row of a relational table, an attribute can have more than one value

questions.formula_note: First Normal Form (1NF): all attribute values in a relational table must be atomic (single, indivisible); multivalued attributes from ER model cannot directly appear in relational tables.

questions.question:
Consider an Entity-Relationship (ER) model in which entity sets E1, E2 and E3 participate in relationship sets R12 and R13. The cardinalities of R12 (between E1 and E2) is a:m and R13 (between E1 and E3) is 1:n (1 on the side of E1 and n on the side of E3). If there are no partial participation constraints, what is the minimum number of tables required in a relational database that faithfully captures the ER model?
A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 5
questions.to_find: Minimum number of relational tables to represent E1, E2, E3 with R12 (a:m, many-to-many) and R13 (1:n, one-to-many from E1 to E3)

understand.plain: We have three entities E1, E2, E3. R12 is a many-to-many relationship between E1 and E2 (a:m means many-to-many). R13 is a one-to-many relationship between E1 (one side) and E3 (many side). We need to find the minimum number of relational tables. No partial participation means no total participation constraints that might allow further merging.

keywords.explain: Both participating entities can be associated with multiple instances of the other. Requires a separate junction table.
keywords.example: Students and Courses: one student enrolls in many courses and one course has many students.

keywords.explain: One entity instance on the '1' side is associated with multiple instances on the 'n' side. Absorbed into the 'n' side table via FK.
keywords.example: One department (E1) has many employees (E3); E3 table stores E1's PK as FK.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='205' y='65' width='70' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>E1</text><rect x='10' y='65' width='70' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='45' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>E2</text><rect x='440' y='65' width='70' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='475' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>E3</text><polygon points='120,65 155,90 120,115 85,90' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>R12</text><polygon points='360,65 395,90 360,115 325,90' class='hi'/><text x='360' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>R13</text><line x1='80' y1='90' x2='85' y2='90' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='155' y1='90' x2='205' y2='90' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='275' y1='90' x2='325' y2='90' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='395' y1='90' x2='440' y2='90' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='120' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>a:m (M:N)</text><text x='360' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1:n (1:N)</text></svg>

given.aim: Find the minimum number of relational tables for E1, E2, E3 with R12 (M:N) and R13 (1:N from E1 to E3).

terms.meaning: Participates in both R12 and R13; needs its own table.

terms.term: R12 (a:m = M:N between E1 and E2)
terms.meaning: Many-to-many requires a separate junction table. E2's table is also needed separately.
terms.connects: Adds 2 tables (E2 table + junction table) — unless E2 can be merged into junction.

terms.term: R13 (1:n, one-to-many from E1 to E3)
terms.meaning: E1 is '1', E3 is 'n'. R13 can be absorbed into E3's table by adding e1_id as FK in E3's table.
terms.connects: E3 table absorbs R13; no extra table for R13.

given.plan: E1 needs its own table. R12 (M:N) needs a junction table; E2 can be merged into this junction table (if E2 appears only in R12). E3 needs its own table with E1's FK for R13. Total = Table_E1 + Table_R12_E2 + Table_E3 = 3.

steps.title: Table for E1
steps.apply: E1 is a central entity participating in both relationships. Table_E1(e1_id, ...) is required. Count = 1.
steps.note: E1 cannot be merged into either relationship without losing data.

steps.title: Handle R13 (1:n from E1 to E3)
steps.apply: R13 is 1:n with E1 on the '1' side and E3 on the 'n' side. Add e1_id as FK into Table_E3. No separate table for R13. Table_E3(e3_id, e1_id, ...). Count = 2.
steps.note: 1:N relationship does not require a new table; FK in the 'many' side suffices.

steps.title: Handle R12 (a:m = M:N between E1 and E2)
steps.formula_raw: M:N -> junction table; leaf entity can merge in
steps.apply: R12 is many-to-many. A junction table Table_R12 is mandatory. If E2 appears only in R12, we can merge E2's attributes into Table_R12, making it Table_R12_E2(e1_id, e2_id, e2_attrs...). This keeps count at 3 (not 4). Count = 3.
steps.note: If E2 is a 'leaf' entity (only in R12), merging saves a table. The minimum is 3.

steps.title: Final count
steps.apply: Table_E1 + Table_E3(with e1_id FK) + Table_R12_E2 = 3 tables.
steps.note: Minimum is 3. Answer is B.

questions.formula_note: ER-to-relational conversion: 1:N absorbed by FK in 'many' side; M:N always requires junction table; leaf entities participating only in one M:N can be merged into that junction table to minimize total tables.
